1: YHWH’s opening question about the mountain goats and hinds giving birth establishes the fundamental christological frame of the divine speeches: the Creator has intimate knowledge of and care for every creature he made. Colossians 1:16: “by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible.” The God who attends the wild birth of mountain goats in remote ranges is the God who became one of his creatures at Bethlehem — the Incarnation is the ultimate expression of Creator-attentiveness to creaturely life.
2: Counting the months of wild pregnancy — the divine arithmetic of creaturely gestation. Matthew 10:29: “are not two sparrows sold for a penny? And not one of them will fall to the ground apart from your Father.” The sparrow-accounting is the NT echo of the mountain-goat-accounting; Christ reveals that divine attention to creaturely life is not a peripheral attribute of God but belongs to the Father’s character that the Son makes known (John 1:18: “the only God, who is at the Father’s side, he has made him known”).
3: The wild creatures bringing forth their young and delivering their offspring — the drama of wild birth without human attendance. John 16:21: “when a woman is giving birth, she has sorrow because her hour has come, but when she has delivered the baby, she no longer remembers the anguish, for joy that a human being has been born into the world.” Christ uses the birth metaphor to describe the eschatological labor of the church; the wild creature’s unattended birth is also the type of Christ’s own birth in the manger, without the attendance of palace or physician.
4: Their young becoming strong and going out, leaving and not returning — the natural independence of wild offspring. John 8:36: “if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed.” The freedom of the wild creature that grows up and departs is the type of the freedom that Christ brings: the children of God, set free by the Son, are not confined to the structures of the old creation’s bondage but go out into the freedom Christ won.
5: Who has let the wild donkey (onager) go free? Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey? — the untamed freedom of the wild ass. Romans 8:20–21: “for the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope that the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God.” The wild donkey’s freedom from human bondage is a foretaste of creation’s eschatological liberation that Christ’s redemption inaugurates.
6: YHWH making the wilderness the wild donkey’s home and the salt land its dwelling — the hostile desert as habitat, not prison. Luke 4:1: “Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan and was led by the Spirit in the wilderness for forty days.” Christ in the wilderness does not flee the hostile environment but inhabits it as the new Adam who will not yield to the tempter. The salt-land as home for the wild free creature is the type of Christ dwelling in the desert of human suffering.
7: The wild donkey scorning the city’s tumult and not hearing the driver’s shout — radical independence from human control. John 18:36: “my kingdom is not of this world. If my kingdom were of this world, my servants would have been fighting.” Christ refuses the political kingdom offered by the tempter (Luke 4:5–8) and scorns the city’s attempt to make him king by force (John 6:15). The wild donkey’s independence from city-tumult is the type of Christ’s kingdom that operates by different logic than earthly power.
8: The wild donkey ranging the hills as pasture, searching for every green thing — the sovereign freedom of creaturely provision. Matthew 6:26: “look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them.” The wild donkey’s hill-ranging for food is the type of the Father’s provision for those who, like Christ, prioritize the kingdom and trust the Father for daily sustenance.
9: Will the wild ox serve you or spend the night at your manger? — the untameable re’em defying domestic service. Philippians 2:7: “taking the form of a servant (doulos, a slave), being born in the likeness of men.” The wild ox that refuses the manger is the anti-type of Christ who voluntarily went to a manger (Luke 2:7) — not in servitude to human power but in the voluntary self-lowering of the Incarnation. The untameable God became tameable in the only way that matters: through love, not compulsion.
10: Can you bind the wild ox in the furrow or will it harrow the valleys after you? — the impossibility of harnessing untamed power for human work. Ephesians 1:19–20: “the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead.” The power that cannot be bound in a furrow is the power that raised Christ from the dead and is now working in those who are in him.
11: Will you rely on the wild ox’s great strength and leave your labor to it? — the folly of depending on undomesticated power. John 15:5: “I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.” The labor that cannot be left to the untameable wild ox can be entrusted to Christ, the Vine, who transmits his life to the branches and bears fruit through those connected to him.
12: Will the wild ox bring your grain home and gather it to your threshing floor? — the wild animal that will not harvest for the human economy. Matthew 3:12: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn.” Christ, not a wild animal, is the one who gathers the harvest: the gathering to the heavenly barn is not entrusted to untameable creaturely force but to the Son who comes in judgment to gather his own.
13: The ostrich’s wing flapping joyfully — the wing of feathers without the stork’s wisdom or care. In the wisdom hierarchy of Job 38–39, the ostrich is notable as the creature from whom wisdom is specifically withheld (v17). In the NT, wisdom is specifically concentrated and given: 1 Corinthians 1:30 (“Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God”); Colossians 2:3 (“in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge”). The creature that lacks wisdom is the anti-type of Christ who is wisdom itself.
14: The ostrich leaving her eggs in the ground for the earth to warm them — the strange parenting inversion. Isaiah 49:15: “can a woman forget her nursing child, that she should have no compassion on the son of her womb? Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you.” The ostrich’s apparently careless maternal behavior highlights by contrast the divine maternal tenderness that reaches its fullest expression in Christ: “Jerusalem, Jerusalem…how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings” (Matthew 23:37).
15: The ostrich forgetting that a foot may crush the eggs or a wild beast trample them — creaturely vulnerability without protective wisdom. John 10:28: “I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.” The eggs left unprotected on the ground are the anti-type of Christ’s sheep who are held in his hand and the Father’s hand — double protection that no foot can trample.
16: The ostrich dealing harshly with her young as though they were not hers — the maternal-inversion creature. Lamentations 4:3: “even jackals offer the breast; they nurse their young, but the daughter of my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the wilderness.” The cruel city and the ostrich-mother are the backdrop for the tender father-figure of Christ: “see what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God” (1 John 3:1). The maternal rejection that the ostrich embodies is the opposite of the Incarnation’s embrace.
17: “God has made her forget wisdom and has not given her a share in understanding” — wisdom withheld from the ostrich by divine sovereign distribution. 1 Corinthians 1:27: “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise.” The sovereign distribution of wisdom — withheld from the ostrich, poured out through the Spirit on the disciples at Pentecost (Acts 2:17) — is the divine prerogative. The wisdom denied to the ostrich is the wisdom given to the weak and foolish in Christ (1 Corinthians 2:12).
18: When the ostrich rouses herself and runs, she laughs at the horse and rider — the compensation of speed for lack of wisdom. 1 Corinthians 1:25: “the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than horses.” The ostrich’s speed surpassing horse and rider is the type of the divine wisdom that appears foolish (the cross) but outruns every human wisdom-strategy. The laughing ostrich is the type of the God who holds the wise in derision (Psalms 2:4) and whose apparent foolishness triumphs.
19: “Do you give the horse his might? Do you clothe his neck with a mane?” — YHWH as the source of the war horse’s power. Revelation 19:11: “then I saw heaven opened, and behold, a white horse! The one sitting on it is called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he judges and makes war.” The war horse’s divine-given might is the animal type; the white horse of the parousia is the eschatological fulfillment. Christ is both the giver of the horse’s might and the rider of the heavenly horse at the final battle.
20: Making the war horse leap like the locust, its majestic snorting striking terror — the awesome power of the battle horse. Joel 2:4–5: “their appearance is like the appearance of horses, and like war horses they run…like the rumbling of chariots on the tops of mountains.” The locust-horse of Joel’s prophecy and the horses of Revelation 9:7 are the eschatological escalation of Job 39’s war horse: the divine power displayed in creation is concentrated in the final eschatological battle where Christ leads the armies of heaven.
21: Pawing in the valley, exulting in strength, going out to meet weapons — the war horse’s fearless eagerness. Hebrews 12:2: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross.” The war horse that goes out to meet weapons without fear is the type of Christ going to the cross “for the joy set before him” — not retreating from the weapons of suffering and death but meeting them directly.
22: Laughing at fear and not being dismayed, not turning back from the sword — the courage of the war horse. John 10:18: “no one takes it [my life] from me, but I lay it down of my own accord.” The war horse that does not flinch from the sword is the type of Christ who set his face toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51) and went voluntarily to the death that would accomplish redemption. The fearlessness is not ignorance of danger but sovereign willingness to face it.
23: The quiver rattling against the horse, the spear and javelin flashing — the instruments of war that the courageous horse ignores. Isaiah 53:5: “he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities.” The spear that the war horse faces was literally planted in Christ’s side at Golgotha (John 19:34). The weapons that the horse faces bravely are the instruments of Christ’s passion; the war horse’s courage typifies the courage of the Servant who did not turn from the smiting (Isaiah 50:6).
24: Swallowing the ground with fierceness and rage, not standing still at the trumpet’s sound — the war horse’s total commitment at the charge. Revelation 19:14–15: “the armies of heaven, arrayed in fine linen, white and pure, were following him on white horses. From his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations.” The war horse’s absolute commitment at the battle charge finds its fullest expression in Christ leading the armies of heaven in the final decisive battle against evil.
25: “Ha!” the horse says at the trumpet sound; smelling the battle from afar, the thunder of captains and the shouting — the war horse’s instinct for conflict. 1 Thessalonians 4:16: “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God.” The trumpet that sends the war horse charging is the type of the trumpet that heralds Christ’s parousia — the divine battle-call that sets in motion the final overthrow of all that opposes the kingdom.
26: “Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars and spreads his wings toward the south?” — the hawk’s migratory instinct beyond human teaching. Deuteronomy 32:11: “like an eagle that stirs up its nest, that flutters over its young, spreading out its wings, catching them, bearing them on its pinions.” God as eagle-parent is the OT background for Christ gathering Jerusalem’s children under his wings (Matthew 23:37). The soaring hawk typifies the divine pastoral care that watches over and gathers the vulnerable.
27: “Is it at your command that the eagle mounts up and makes his nest on high?” — the eagle’s ascent beyond human command. Ephesians 4:10: “he who descended is the one who also ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things.” The eagle ascending to make its nest on high is the type of Christ’s ascension: he who descended (the Incarnation) is the one who ascended “far above all the heavens.” The eagle’s high nest is the type of the session at the Father’s right hand.
28: The eagle dwelling on the rock, making its stronghold on the rocky crag — the impregnable height of the eagle’s refuge. Matthew 16:18: “on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it.” The eagle’s rocky stronghold is the type of the church built on Christ the rock — equally impregnable from below. 1 Corinthians 10:4: “that rock was Christ” — the rock that the eagle inhabits is the rock that is Christ himself.
29: From the height the eagle spies out the prey, its eyes seeing from afar — the eagle’s panoramic vision. Hebrews 4:13: “no creature is hidden from God’s sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account.” Revelation 1:14: “his eyes were like a flame of fire” — the eyes of the risen Christ that see from afar. The eagle’s far-seeing eyes from the high crag are the type of Christ’s all-seeing gaze from the Father’s right hand.
30: The eagle’s young ones suck up blood, and where the slain are, there is the eagle — the eagle as gatherer at the place of death. Matthew 24:28: “wherever the corpse is, there the vultures will gather.” This sign of judgment (the gathering of scavengers at the place of death) is Christ’s own sign for the parousia’s irreversible judgment. Revelation 19:17–18: the angel calls the birds to the great supper of God where the dead of the final battle are consumed. The eagle at the slain is the sign of final divine judgment executed by Christ.
